8. Scattering in 3D

8.1. Scattering Amplitudes

Definition 10. A collision between particles is called scattering if the initial and final state particles are the same. The scattering is elastic if none of the initial states of the particles change in the collision.

Assumptions:

  • No spin
  • Nonrelativistic
  • Interaction potential is translationally invariant, i.e. .
  • We will assume the potential is either finite with range or decays faster than .

Let denote the reduced mass of the system. The Hamiltonian is

(91)

Far away the solutions take the form

(92)

where , and solves the TISE:

(93)

How do we enforce scattering solutions? Recall in 1D, we had an incident wave plus a scattering wave , including both reflected and transmitted wave for . In 3D, our ansatz for large is an incident wave plus a radial scattering wave:

(94)

We call the scattering amplitude. Note that in zero potential solves the Schrodinger equation, but does not. This approaches an exact solution of Equation 93 for .

Before deriving , let’s consider observables.

  • Classically, we imagine the differential cross section as the area of the portion of the beam that will scatter in the direction of a given solid angle (8.223). More generally (in classical and quantum), we define as the area that would remove from the incident beam the particles scattered into the solid angle . We have

    (95)

From Equation 94, the denominator is given by . The numerator is . We obtain

(96)

The total cross section represents the area that removes from the incoming beam a flux of particles equal to the flux of the scattered particles. This does not concern the location of the actual area in the beam.

8.2. Central potential

Now let’s solve Equation 93 for a central potential. We have azimuthal symmetry so drops from .

First, we rewrite the plane wave in the -direction via spherical coordinates:

Lemma 2 (Free Particle Radial Wavefunction). If , then the solution to the radial Schrodinger equation takes the form

(97)

where and are spherical Bessel functions.

For we have

(98)

For and we have

(99)

Proposition 14 (Rayleigh Formula).

(100)

where is the spherical Bessel function.

As , we get the superposition of partial spherical waves propagating inwards and outwards (Eq 30.3.6):

(101)

Remarks about the formula

  • Since we have azimuthal symmetry, , so the wavefunctions do not fit into the expansion as each spherical harmonic has the form

    (102)

In analogy to 1D scattering off a wall, we can write the solution for as incoming waves plus reflected wave, except this time we have multiple partial waves for each . When we turn on , we add a phase shift to the outgoing wave. Starting at Eq 30.3.8 they show this is equivalent to the scattering ansatz (Equation 94). The ansatz with phase shift becomes

Idea 2 (Ansatz for scattered waves). Replace the outgoing wavefunction in Equation 101 with

(103)

Matching with Equation 94 we obtain

(104)

Why does this work? The general radial solution for is

(105)

Reading off the equation, we see that each partial wave matches the form in Equation 101 up to global phase if we let . Matching partial waves is enough since the spherical harmonics are orthonormal, hence the probability amplitude is unchanged.

Integrating the differential cross section and applying orthonormality of s, we obtain

(106)

Remarks

  • For each , the partial wavefunctions are bounded above:

    (107)

  • We have an interesting identity:

    Theorem 7 (Optical Theorem).

    (108)

    Intuitively, the net flux out of the sphere must be zero by conservation of probability (since we’re starting with a plane wave), and so the shadow in the forward direction, which turns out to be related to must be added to the radial flux.

  • For , the spherical bessel functions equal evaluate to make the wavefunction exactly equal

    (109)

    which is exactly analogous to the scattering on the half-line ansatz.

8.3. Computing the Phase Shift

Now that we have this nice ansatz for each partial wave, how do we go about computing each ? Here is the strategy:

  1. Recall solutions restricted to a single partial wave take the form

    (110)

  2. Match the solutions inside the potential to the partial waves to solve for the ratio .

For non-spherically symmetric potentials, our partial wave formalism doesn’t apply.

How do we determine Green’s function?

Proposition 17. Green’s function for has solutions

(114)

is called the retarded G.F. and corresponds to outgoing waves. is the advanced G.F. and corresponds to incoming waves.
